title: Opening Statement to the Inquiry into Schemes, Digital Wallets and Innovation in the Payments Sector
summary: Ellis Connolly, Head of Payments Policy at the RBA, testified before the House of Representatives Standing Committee on Economics regarding the RBA's expanded mandate under amendments to the Payment Systems (Regulation) Act 1998 passed in September 2025. The RBA plans consultations in mid-2026 on three-party card schemes, mobile wallets, BNPL providers, and e-commerce platforms. Additionally, the RBA is working on modernizing account-to-account payments and exploring tokenized asset markets through Project Acacia.
key_points: RBA's mandate expanded under September 2025 amendments to the Payment Systems (Regulation) Act 1998 to cover a broader range of payment systems and participants
RBA will consult in mid-2026 on priority issues including three-party card schemes, mobile wallets, BNPL providers, and e-commerce platforms
The amendments are part of broader reforms including a proposed licensing regime for payments and digital asset reforms
RBA is engaging with Treasury and industry on future design of Australia's account-to-account payments system for modernization

Project Acacia is exploring digital money and financial infrastructure to support tokenised asset markets in Australia, undertaken alongside industry, regulators, and the Digital Finance Cooperative Research Centre
why_it_matters: Consider adding that this is an opening statement for a parliamentary inquiry, which means it sets the RBA's initial position but does not constitute final policy. The mid-2026 consultations will be the substantive opportunity for stakeholder input.
market_structure_context: The testimony discusses structural reforms to Australia's payment system including: (1) expanded regulatory remit under amended PSRA covering new payment participants and systems; (2) upcoming consultations on three-party card schemes, mobile wallets, BNPL and e-commerce platforms; (3) account-to-account payments modernization workstream; (4) Project Acacia exploring tokenised asset market infrastructure. The source outlines the regulatory framework but does not quantify balance sheet implications or specify implementation timelines beyond mid-2026 consultations. As an opening statement, this testimony establishes the RBA's preliminary position for the inquiry rather than announcing finalized policy.

notes: This is a congressional testimony by an RBA official regarding payments system regulation, not a monetary policy or macro catalyst event. The source discusses regulatory framework and future consultations but contains no rate decisions, economic forecasts, or market-moving data. Classified as 'market structure' given its focus on payments infrastructure. Project Acacia references tokenised asset exploration but no concrete implementation details or timelines are provided.
